active_paradigms.idea: Extend the current logistic RHS so the inhibitor also delays the primary-nucleation source, not only the autocatalytic feedback
active_paradigms.description: Keep the proven mass-conserving logistic core (c6 - c)*(source + feedback_eff*c). The dominant unmodeled failure is that heavily-inhibited (high cd) curves have much longer lag phases, but the current source term c0^2 + c1^2*monomer breaks the lag identically for all cd, so worst-case delayed curves are underfit and inflate the high-quantile shape loss. Divide the SOURCE by an inhibitor factor as well: source_eff = (c0^2 + c1^2*monomer) / (1 + c4^2*inhibitor). Reuse the SAME c4 for both source and feedback suppression to avoid over-parameterization, keeping the total constants at 7. So feedback_eff = c2^2*monomer*(1+c3^2*seed) / (1 + c4^2*inhibitor + c5^2*inhibitor*concentration) and source_eff = (c0^2+c1^2*monomer)/(1+c4^2*inhibitor). At cd=0 both factors collapse to 1, exactly recovering the uninhibited law and preserving the strong existing fit. This directly pushes the 10/25/50% crossing times later for high-cd curves, targeting the worst-curve shape term. All denominators are 1 + nonnegative, so no singularities; all rates squared for positivity. Keep initial_values as seven 1.0 entries.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: heldout_curve_shape_loss (especially worst-curve high-quantile term on delayed high-cd curves) while preserving heldout_curve_nmse
active_paradigms.cautions: Do not add a new constant for the source suppression — reuse c4 to keep fitting stable on small datasets. Verify cd=0 curves still collapse the factor to exactly 1. Keep the (c6 - c) sink unchanged.

active_paradigms.idea: Add a higher-order (concentration-squared) secondary-nucleation term to sharpen the sigmoid inflection and better match slope profiles
active_paradigms.description: The current feedback is linear in concentration (feedback*c), giving a symmetric logistic that may under-sharpen the steep rise seen after long lags in inhibited Abeta42 curves. Knowles secondary nucleation scales with existing aggregate mass to a superlinear power. Instead of a variable/fractional exponent (numerically risky), add a bounded, stable c^2 autocatalytic contribution alongside the existing c^1 term: growth = c2^2*monomer*(1+c3^2*seed)*concentration + c6^2*concentration**2, then divide the whole growth by the inhibitor factor (1 + c4^2*inhibitor + c5^2*inhibitor*concentration). RHS = (c7 - concentration)*(c0^2 + c1^2*monomer + growth_eff). The concentration**2 term cannot overflow because concentration stays in [0, c7] driven by the (c7-c) sink, so it is bounded and smooth for odeint. This gives a self-accelerating rise whose steepness is data-tunable, improving the slope-profile part of the shape loss. Uses 8 constants (well under 13). Keep initial_values as eight 1.0 entries; c7 initialized at 1.0 (plateau just above normalized 1).
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: shape_loss slope-profile component and NMSE on the steep rise phase of sharp sigmoid curves
active_paradigms.cautions: concentration**2 is only safe because the (c7-c) sink bounds c; ensure c7 initial value >= 1 so capacity stays positive early. Do not let both c^1 and c^2 feedback coefficients grow unbounded — squared coefficients plus the sink keep it stable. Confirm eighth constant does not destabilize least-squares on the smallest datasets.

active_paradigms.idea: Replace the linear inhibitor divisor with a saturable Langmuir occupancy factor that suppresses feedback as bound-site fraction
active_paradigms.description: Physically, surface-binding inhibitors occupy a finite number of secondary-nucleation sites, so their effect saturates at high cd rather than growing linearly. Model the active (unblocked) fraction of the autocatalytic pathway as a Langmuir free-fraction: free_fraction = 1 / (1 + c4^2*inhibitor) but multiply feedback by this AND keep an occupancy-dependent extra slowdown that scales with the current aggregate: feedback_eff = feedback * (1 / (1 + c4^2*inhibitor)) - here the divisor form already saturates, so the structural change is to make the inhibitor act as a MULTIPLICATIVE free-fraction on both feedback and the seed-catalyzed term while REMOVING the concentration-coupled term c5^2*inhibitor*concentration (which the divisor makes redundant). Concretely: inhib_free = 1/(1 + c4^2*inhibitor); feedback_eff = c2^2*monomer*(1 + c3^2*seed)*inhib_free; RHS = (c5 - concentration)*(c0^2 + c1^2*monomer*inhib_free + feedback_eff*concentration). This makes cd suppress BOTH primary and secondary pathways with a single saturable, bounded factor, uses only 6 constants (fewer than current 7 → more stable fitting), collapses to uninhibited law at cd=0, and never produces singularities since the denominator is 1+nonnegative.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: robustness across all inhibitor datasets via fewer, better-conditioned constants; improved fit on high-cd saturating-suppression curves
active_paradigms.cautions: Fewer constants may slightly underfit some datasets — verify aggregate combined_score does not drop below current best. Ensure c5 (plateau) initial value covers normalized range; keep it at 1.0. Confirm removing the concentration-coupled inhibitor term does not lose the timing-delay behavior that the worst-curve shape term rewards.

tried_paradigms.idea: Autocatalytic Knowles-Cohen mass-action RHS with inhibitor-modulated secondary nucleation (remove explicit-time fudge term)
tried_paradigms.description: Replace the non-physical `-c8*t*c` term with a proper autonomous autocatalytic structure derived from the amyloid master equation. Set capacity = (plateau - concentration) representing remaining monomer pool. Model growth = capacity * (primary + secondary) where primary = c0^2 + c1^2*monomer (monomer-dependent primary nucleation, stays finite at c=0 to seed the lag) and secondary = c2^2*(monomer + c3^2*seed)*concentration**2 (self-amplifying autocatalysis, quadratic in c drives sharp sigmoidal burst matching 10-25-50 percent crossings). Inhibitor enters as differential suppression: divide secondary by (1 + c4^2*inhibitor) but leave primary lightly damped by (1 + c5^2*inhibitor) so cd primarily slows the autocatalytic burst (biologically the dominant inhibitor target) while preserving cd=0 -> denominators=1. Final: expression = capacity*(primary/(1+c5^2*inhibitor) + secondary/(1+c4^2*inhibitor)). Use ~8 squared constants (c0..c7 with plateau=c6, and c7 as an extra secondary monomer coefficient). initial_values all 1.0 except plateau ~1.0. This is autonomous, smooth, positive, no singularities since denominators >=1 and concentration bounded in [0,1].
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: heldout_curve_shape_loss (lag/burst timing at 10-25-50 percent crossings) and heldout_curve_nmse simultaneously; the quadratic-in-c autocatalysis sharpens the sigmoid to match slope profile without the fragile explicit-time term
tried_paradigms.cautions: concentration**2 with capacity keeps growth bounded (goes to 0 at plateau) so no overflow. Keep constants squared for positivity. Avoid adding both linear and quadratic c terms unless needed; start quadratic-only to test whether burst sharpness improves. Watch that removing t*c does not regress datasets that relied on it for a slow decay tail — if plateau overshoot appears, the (plateau-c) capacity should self-correct.

tried_paradigms.idea: Hill-type saturable inhibitor kinetics with monomer-order growth exponent surrogate
tried_paradigms.description: Model the inhibitor effect as a saturable (Hill/Michaelis-Menten) suppression rather than the current linear-in-cd denominator, which cannot saturate at high inhibitor. Define growth = capacity * source * secondary_factor where capacity = plateau - concentration, source = c0^2 + c1^2*monomer + c2^2*seed. Represent effective inhibitor blocking with a bounded saturating factor efficacy = 1/(1 + c3^2*inhibitor/(c4^2 + inhibitor)) — note dividing inhibitor by (c4^2+inhibitor) makes suppression saturate as cd grows, matching dose-response plateaus of stoichiometric inhibitors, and equals 1 exactly at cd=0. Multiply the autocatalytic branch: secondary_factor = 1 + c5^2*monomer*concentration*(1+c6^2*seed). Full RHS = capacity*source*efficacy*secondary_factor. This uses ~7 constants. The saturating Hill form is smooth everywhere (denominator c4^2+inhibitor > 0 always, and inner denominator 1+... >= 1), globally defined, and captures the empirical observation that beyond a threshold inhibitor concentration additional inhibitor gives diminishing kinetic delay.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: robustness across high-cd datasets (worst-curve quantile term in shape_loss) by letting inhibitor effect saturate instead of exploding the denominator; improves per-dataset consistency and reduces the penalized hard-case shape components
tried_paradigms.cautions: Ensure c4^2 + inhibitor never zero — since inhibitor>=0 and c4^2>=0, add nothing needed but if a dataset has cd=0 the ratio inhibitor/(c4^2+inhibitor)=0 cleanly giving efficacy=1. Guard against c4 fitting to ~0 causing a step-like response near small cd; the outer 1/(1+...) softens this. Keep total constants under 8 to avoid unstable least-squares on small datasets.

tried_paradigms.idea: Gompertz-type growth structure using log-capacity for asymmetric sigmoid with inhibitor-scaled rate
tried_paradigms.description: Switch the saturation mechanism from logistic (plateau - c) to a Gompertz form, which produces an asymmetric sigmoid (slower approach to plateau, earlier inflection) that often fits protein aggregation lag-dominated curves better than symmetric logistic. Implement the Gompertz rate smoothly as capacity_g = concentration * (c0^2 - sp.log(concentration + c1^2)) where the small positive offset c1^2 keeps the log finite and real for concentration in [0,1] (argument = c + c1^2 >= c1^2 > 0, strictly safe). The multiplicative factor concentration provides autocatalytic self-limitation and vanishes at c=0, so combine with a small seed/monomer-driven initiation source0 = c2^2 + c3^2*monomer + c4^2*seed to escape the c=0 fixed point. Inhibitor scales the whole rate: expression = (source0 + c5^2*capacity_g) / (1 + c6^2*inhibitor + c7^2*inhibitor*concentration). About 8 constants. Gompertz is standard in bacterial/aggregation growth modeling and its asymmetry directly targets the 75-90 percent crossing timing that symmetric logistic mis-times.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: shape_loss specifically at the 75 percent and 90 percent response crossings and the upper part of the slope profile, where Gompertz asymmetry corrects the too-symmetric logistic tail of the current program
tried_paradigms.cautions: log argument must stay strictly positive: concentration + c1^2 with c1 squared and concentration >= 0 is safe, but if the integrator briefly overshoots concentration slightly negative, use concentration**2 or ensure c1^2 >= a small floor by initializing c1 ~ 0.3 (c1^2=0.09). log(small) is large-negative — multiplied by concentration near 0 it vanishes, avoiding blowup. Test that odeint does not produce NaN; if instability appears, replace sp.log(c+c1^2) with sp.log((c+c1^2)/(1+c1^2)) to bound the argument near 1.

tried_paradigms.idea: Autocatalytic secondary-nucleation logistic RHS with inhibitor-suppressed rate (Knowles master-equation reduction)
tried_paradigms.description: Replace the non-autonomous time term with true autocatalysis so the model reproduces the sigmoidal lag phase measured by the shape-loss crossing times. Set capacity = plateau - concentration with plateau = c0 (near 1, so keep initial_value 1.0). Build a growth rate that combines primary nucleation (seed/monomer-driven, active even at c=0 to break the lag) and secondary nucleation (proportional to concentration, the autocatalytic engine): rate = c1**2 * seed + c2**2 * monomer + c3**2 * monomer * concentration + c4**2 * concentration**2. Divide the whole growth by an inhibitor factor inhib = 1 + c5**2 * inhibitor + c6**2 * inhibitor * concentration to encode dose-dependent suppression of both elongation and secondary nucleation while preserving cd=0 behavior (factor -> 1). Final expression = capacity * rate / inhib. This is autonomous (no explicit x0), 7 constants, all squared for positivity/stability. Use initial_values=[1.0,0.5,0.5,0.5,0.5,0.5,0.5]. The concentration**2 term is the secondary-nucleation autocatalytic feedback that creates the delayed steep rise the worst-curve shape term penalizes.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: heldout_curve_shape_loss (lag/crossing timing at 10/25/50/75/90%) and dataset_nmse simultaneously via correct sigmoidal shape
tried_paradigms.cautions: Keep plateau slightly above 1 by fitting; capacity can go negative if plateau<c, but c is normalized to [0,1] so with plateau~1 this stays bounded. All rate constants squared to prevent negative/oscillatory growth. Do not add explicit time dependence — autonomy is what makes multi-start integration stable.

tried_paradigms.idea: Hill-type saturable inhibitor damping on a monomer-depletion (mass-conserving) autocatalytic core
tried_paradigms.description: Model aggregation as mass transfer from a monomer pool: growth is proportional to available monomer capacity (plateau - concentration) and a self-catalytic term, but inhibitor acts as a saturable Hill-type brake rather than a linear divisor, matching how small-molecule inhibitors saturate at high dose. Define capacity = c0 - concentration. Define auto = c1**2 + c2**2*concentration + c3**2*concentration**2 (nucleation baseline + linear + quadratic autocatalysis). Define seed_source = c4**2*seed + c5**2*monomer. Define inhibitor damping using a bounded rational Hill term: damp = 1/(1 + c6**2*inhibitor/(c7**2 + concentration)) which increases suppression when inhibitor is high and concentration is low (early lag extension) yet saturates smoothly (denominator strictly positive since c7**2>=0 plus a small guard, and concentration>=0). Final expression = capacity * (seed_source + auto) * damp. Eight constants. Use initial_values=[1.0,0.3,0.3,0.3,0.3,0.3,0.3,0.5]. This targets inhibitor curves that show delayed onset which the high-quantile worst-curve shape term specifically penalizes.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: worst-curve (high-quantile) shape_loss on strongly-inhibited (high cd) trajectories and overall scored_loss robustness
tried_paradigms.cautions: Add a small positive constant inside the Hill denominator via c7**2 initialized to 0.5 so 1/(c7**2+concentration) never blows up (concentration>=0 already guarantees positivity but keep c7 nonzero-init). Avoid making damp itself unbounded; the 1/(1+...) form keeps it in (0,1]. Confirm cd=0 gives damp=1 exactly, preserving non-inhibited baseline.

tried_paradigms.idea: Gompertz-form growth (log-capacity) for asymmetric sigmoid with inhibitor-modulated intrinsic rate
tried_paradigms.description: Abeta ThT curves are often asymmetric sigmoids with a sharp early rise and slow saturation, better captured by a Gompertz law dc/dt = rate * c * log(plateau/c) than by symmetric logistic. Use a numerically safe surrogate: define capacity_log = log((c0 + eps) / (concentration + eps)) is risky, so instead use the smooth polynomial-Gompertz surrogate capacity = concentration*(c0 - concentration) blended with a skew term concentration*(c0-concentration)**2 to introduce asymmetry. Concretely: growth = c1**2*seed + c2**2*monomer + c3**2*concentration ; skew = 1 + c4**2*(c0 - concentration) ; inhib = 1 + c5**2*inhibitor*(1 + c6**2*concentration). Final expression = concentration_term where core = (c0 - concentration) * growth * skew / inhib, and add a small monomer-driven primary-nucleation seed term c7**2*monomer*(c0-concentration)/inhib so growth is nonzero when concentration starts near 0 (avoids a stuck flat solution). Final expression = (c0 - concentration)*(growth*skew + c7**2*monomer)/inhib. Eight constants, fully polynomial/rational and finite everywhere on [0,1]. initial_values=[1.0,0.3,0.3,0.3,0.3,0.3,0.3,0.3].
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: dataset_nmse via asymmetric saturation matching plus shape crossing-time accuracy at 75%/90% (slow-saturation tail)
tried_paradigms.cautions: Avoid actual log() to eliminate domain errors when concentration approaches 0 or exceeds plateau during odeint transients; the polynomial skew term reproduces Gompertz asymmetry without singularities. Keep c0 near 1 so capacity is positive across normalized range. The c7 primary-nucleation term is essential to seed growth from near-zero initial concentration; without it the RHS is ~0 at c=0 and integration stays flat.

tried_paradigms.idea: Autocatalytic self-accelerating lag ODE with inhibitor delaying the feedback (not just amplitude), via pysr_harness.evaluate_expression
tried_paradigms.description: Keep the logistic capacity but make growth genuinely autocatalytic so the model produces a lag phase followed by a sharp rise, which directly improves the shape loss (slope profile and 10/25/50/75/90% crossing timing). Set rate = source + feedback*concentration**2, where source = c0**2 + c1**2*monomer is a small nucleation seed and feedback = c2**2*monomer*(1 + c3**2*seed) is the autocatalytic term whose square-of-c dependence guarantees positivity. The inhibitor must delay the crossing times, so divide ONLY the autocatalytic feedback by an inhibitor factor: feedback_eff = feedback/(1 + c4**2*inhibitor + c5**2*inhibitor*concentration). Then capacity = c6 - concentration and expression = capacity*(source + feedback_eff*concentration). Drop the non-physical explicit-time term entirely (t appearing in RHS breaks autonomy and hurt earlier Knowles attempts only because feedback wasn't present). Use 7 constants, initial_values all 1.0 except c6=1.0. This gives a mass-conserving self-limiting sigmoid whose inflection point shifts later with higher inhibitor, matching delayed inhibitor curves and reducing the worst-curve shape term.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: heldout_curve_shape_loss (crossing timing on delayed inhibitor curves) while keeping nmse; targets the 0.25 shape weight and worst-curve high-quantile term
tried_paradigms.cautions: Ensure c6 (plateau) can exceed 1 so capacity stays positive on normalized data; keep all rate coefficients as c**2 for positivity so odeint stays stable; concentration**2 feedback is bounded because capacity forces c toward plateau, no overflow. Exactly one evaluate_expression call, straight-line, return directly.

tried_paradigms.idea: Additive inhibitor time-shift on autocatalysis using a saturating monomer-consumption source and a bounded rational suppression targeting crossing-time percentiles
tried_paradigms.description: Reformulate so the inhibitor acts as an additive resistance in the denominator that scales with remaining monomer, mimicking how inhibitors sequester monomer and push the 25/50/75% crossings later. Define consumed = concentration (progress), available = c0 - concentration (remaining capacity, c0>=1). Set growth = (c1**2 + c2**2*monomer + c3**2*seed*concentration)*available. Inhibitor suppression: denom = 1 + c4**2*inhibitor*(1 + c5**2*(1-concentration)) so suppression is strongest early (during lag, when 1-concentration is large) and weakens as the curve saturates — this specifically stretches the early crossing times without flattening the plateau, directly attacking shape_loss on delayed curves. expression = growth/denom. Use 6 constants, initial_values 1.0 with c0=1.0. This is a compact rational RHS, globally smooth, denominator strictly >=1 so no singularities, and no exponentials/logs so least-squares conditioning is benign.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: shape_loss crossing-timing accuracy at early percentiles (10/25/50%) on high-inhibitor curves; maintain plateau height for nmse
tried_paradigms.cautions: denom bounded below by 1 (all c**2 terms nonnegative) so division is always safe; keep constant count at 6 to avoid over-parameterization on small datasets; verify available stays positive by allowing c0>1 via multi-start scaling. One scorer call, no branching.

tried_paradigms.idea: Two-timescale product ODE separating a fast nucleation source from a slow inhibitor-modulated elongation, replacing the single lumped rate to better match dual-phase kinetic shape
tried_paradigms.description: Abeta aggregation shows a fast initial nucleation burst plus slower fibril elongation; model the RHS as a SUM of two smooth terms rather than one product so the slope profile has the correct two-phase character measured by the shape loss. Term A (elongation/autocatalysis): elong = c0**2*concentration*(c1 - concentration)*monomer/(1 + c2**2*inhibitor). Term B (primary nucleation source, inhibitor-suppressed, monomer-fed, saturating as c grows): nucl = (c3**2 + c4**2*seed)*monomer*(c5 - concentration)/(1 + c6**2*inhibitor*concentration). expression = elong + nucl. Here nucl dominates early (providing lag escape from c near 0 since elong ~ 0 when concentration is tiny) and elong dominates the sharp mid-phase rise, producing the correct asymmetric sigmoid. Inhibitor divides both channels with different sensitivities (c2 vs c6) letting the fit place suppression on whichever phase each dataset needs. Use 7 constants, c1=c5=1.0, others 1.0. Smooth, mass-conserving (both terms vanish at capacity), denominators >=1.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: overall slope profile match across the whole curve (both lag-escape and rise), improving both nmse and shape_loss and reducing worst-curve penalty from datasets needing distinct phase behavior
tried_paradigms.cautions: Two capacity constants c1 and c5 may become redundant — acceptable but monitor; both must be allowed >1 so capacities stay positive; sum of two positive terms avoids sign flips and keeps odeint monotone-increasing which matches saturating data. Keep to one evaluate_expression call and straight-line code.
